speaker: interviewer
text: I need just 30 seconds. You can relax for 30 seconds.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am relaxed. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, do they not do-- I thought on each tape they do this--

speaker: interviewer
text: It should slate it at the beginning of each

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. But we haven't been doing that?

speaker: interviewer
text: It should just reiterate the slate at the beginning of each. We didn't do that, but we should have.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. That's fine. No, no. You usually I think-- yeah. But that's good, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: And slate please.

speaker: interviewee
text: March 28th, 1995. Survivor Ernest Ari Katz. I'm Aaron Katz. The interviewer, Aaron Weiss. Northbrook, Illinois, USA. Language, English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, slate down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hi, I'm Aaron Weiss. A-A-R-O-N W-E-I-S-S. Today is the 28th of March, 1995. I'm conducting an interview with Ernest Katz. And we are in Northbrook, Illinois, USA. Will you please state and spell your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Ernest Katz, spelled E-R-N-E-S-T Katz, K-A-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name at birth was Aaron. And I'm known by the name of Ari.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you spell Aaron and Ari?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-A-R-O-N, Ari, A-R-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: March the 28th, 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably 72.

speaker: interviewer
text: Happy birthday.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city and country of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Velike Loucky, Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you please spell Velike Loucky? V-E-L-I-K-Y, L-U-C- with a [INAUDIBLE] K-Y. And can you describe the community in which you lived in before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: The community was a suburb like of Munkacs, which is located about nine kilometers away, which is about five-- approximately five miles. The population was about 10,000, with about 70 families-- Jewish families, rather.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the economic life? Non-Jewish and Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish was primarily business. And the economic life was farming. And it was a very rich farming area.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there any organizations in Munkacs? Political organizations, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were Zionist organizations in Munkacs, which naturally the Munkacs rebbe that my grandfather was a follower of opposed Zionist organizations. And I was not able to join those organizations.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Velike Loucky?

speaker: interviewee
text: Velike Loucky-- just a few organizations, such the youngsters. Again, I was not part of those.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the political involvement in Velike Loucky?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we were born-- when I was born, and Velike Loucky was Czechoslovakia, it was a very democratic environment. We were all treated equally. We were protected by the government. There was no hostility towards the Jews. A little anti-Semitism crept up here and there, but nothing-- nothing primary that would affect anybody's life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of interaction did you have with the non-Jews of the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good interaction, because we had-- my grandfather had a business, which my mother helped the business. And-- a general store. Also had a saloon, and also manufacturing of sewer pipes type. So we had a good-- good business, and a good interaction with the community.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your family life. Who was in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- we were living in Munkacs when my father left Munkacs. He left Czechoslovakia in 1929 to come to the United States. He went-- he came to the United States for business purposes, to be able to earn a better living, and also for reasons that he had to leave the business in Czechoslovakia at that time. At that time, we were a family-- my mother, myself, my brother, and my sister Magda. That was in 1929. A year later-- actually not a year later, about six months after my father left, my youngest sister was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when he came to the United States, we went to Loucky to live with our grandfather. Our grandfather and grandmother and our family were living together. My mother helped them run the business, and all the aspects. Was that your paternal grandparents or your maternal grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maternal grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were supposed to eventually meet your father in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father went with the idea to bring us out. But my mother was very reluctant at the time, and she was hoping that he would be able to come back, because she was close to the family. She was close to her grandmother. They were not in very good health. And when the decision was made that we should come out, he should send us paper to come to the United States, it was too late already. And we got stuck there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What languages were spoken in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish. A little Hungarian my mother spoke sometimes with her sister. But primarily, we were speaking Yiddish in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What ways did you get the news about what was going on in the world? What newspapers? Did you have a radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had radio already. We had newspapers. And primarily the news was generated when we went to minyan every morning, to the shul. And we sat around the stove, the heated stove, and everybody was talking about the [INAUDIBLE] events, what's going on in the world. Except we knew the good news, what was going on, but towards the end, we had absolutely no idea that what is happening to the Jewish communities throughout Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your family's level of religiosity?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father-- my grandfather was very orthodox. And I was raised in an orthodox way, which means when I grew up and I went to cheder, and to public schools. I had payos, and naturally we were teased by the non-Jews for that, you know, for having payos and stuff like that. But we endured with no problem at all. The life in the house was such that when we were 5 or 6 years-- when I was 5 or 6 years old, we were getting up at 5 o'clock in the morning to go to cheder. It was held in a community house. And by 8:00 o'clock, we had to go to the public schools. Then we came home for lunch. We went back to the public schools. By 3 o'clock we went back to the cheder, and we didn't get home till about 5:00, 6:00 o'clock for dinner. And that was daily occurrence. And Saturday came Shabbos. We went to shul. And after shul, we had the big meal. And after the big meal, my grandfather wanted to know what I learned during the week. And sometimes it didn't go so good, so I got a smack on my nose. And naturally blood started coming out. And that was the end of the examination for that Shabbos, because my mother got a hold of me and washed my face. And I didn't have to go through any more torture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about Shabbos and the religious rituals that you practiced in your home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbos was very, very observant. All the Jews in that community were very observant. But my grandfather in particular, the businesses were closed Friday afternoon. We went to shul. We had the Shabbos meal. We were singing zemiros and so forth. We always had somebody, some poor person that came into the village to make some collections for their sustenance. We had them for the guest for Shabbos. As a matter of fact we had a special room that was held for them for every Shabbos, to have a guest in the house. And then in the morning we went to shul. We came home from shul again. We had a meal. And then the test-- the test was going on that I mentioned before. My grandfather laid down and everybody rested, until Saturday night. It was Shabbos was over. They made havdalah and the businesses were open again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your grandfather and his ties with the rabbi.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather was a very respected man in the community. He was like an elder of the community. He was known by the name of [? Reb ?] Mordechai, which means he had a title of [INAUDIBLE]. He was a follower of the Munkacs [INAUDIBLE]. We had no rabbi in our community. But our community belonged-- I mean, the overseer of the community, the rabbinic overseer of the community, was the Munkacs [INAUDIBLE], which is a very famous [INAUDIBLE], and his name was Rabbi Shapiro. And he occasionally came to visit our community. And when he came to visit our community, he stayed in our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What contact did you have with non-Jewish students, and with Jewish students as friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the school-- the school life for us was very-- a very happy life. As a mother of fact, my present wife, Etta, she was my childhood sweetheart. And we were always picked to perform in the same plays, in the same dances. Somehow we were always paired up together. We had a happy childhood. We had a very good life in those days. And the bad times didn't start till a little later, when we grew up a little already.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the good times-- what did you do for entertainment during the good times?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for entertainment-- there wasn't much time for entertainment, because you had-- you were always busy with studies. Like I mentioned before, the cheder and the public schools, and we were playing on the outside during recess in school-- football, soccer, stuff like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name-- or did the grammar school you go to have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It really didn't have a name, no. It was just known as a Czech school. They also had Russian schools. But primarily they had-- everybody went to the Czech schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of the street that you lived on? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the street that we lived on was Masarykova Ulitsa. That was the name of the founder of Czechoslovakia, was Masaryk. And he was raised in the United States, or grew up in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me a little bit about your high school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we went to school, we went to Czech grammar school. But when we graduated from grammar school, we needed to go to high school, and there was no Czech high school in the area. So we had to cram for a two-month period-- there was no vacation time-- to learn the Russian language, and to read and to write Russian, to be able to be accepted in the Russian high school. And we had accomplished it and we went to Russian high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: During your leisure time, you talked about vacations. If you had leisure time, did you travel at all in Czechoslovakia or Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Not really. We went to visit aunts and uncles not far away from our village. They lived in the area also. And we had experiences where my uncle that lived in Seredna-- or rather my uncle and aunt, they had about eight or nine children. By the way, none of them survived. Not one of them survived. And I remember a case when the Munkacs [INAUDIBLE] also stayed at their house, because of the connection with the grandfather, that the Munkacs [INAUDIBLE] came to visit for a shabbos to my uncle's house. And the entourage came in. And I remember-- I don't know how old I was. As a little boy, I ran out to greet the rabbi. And the shamas was ahead of the-- walking ahead of the rabbi. And I ran over to the shamas and kissed his hand. And the shamas said, no, no, you make a mistake. That's the rabbi behind me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain to people who watch this what the shamas-- who he is?

speaker: interviewee
text: A shamas is the guy that takes care of the rabbi-- of the rabbi's needs, and [INAUDIBLE] in other words.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this time, did you have communication with your father in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. We had communication. My father, I used to write him letters. Mother sent him pictures and letters, and showing him-- he was very concerned how we were growing up, and so forth and so on. And he sent us money to support us-- as much as he could. 1929, I understand, was a very bad year in the United States. So whatever he could, he was sending us to help us out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Up to what year did you continue to read-- to receive information from him, letters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was 1939, 1940. But then it stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you first heard news about the Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we probably heard some rumblings going on and the Nazis during what's going-- what's happening in Germany. But we didn't give it too much credence. The only thing that I know, in 1938, I went to the yeshiva in Dunaszerdahely. I was studying under Rabbi Katz. It was a very famous yeshiva. And in 1939, when the Germans came in and invaded the Sudetenland, they sent everybody home. And this is when we came home. And that's when we know that the problem was starting. But again, we did not know what kind of problems we were going to encounter at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the yeshiva was located in--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] is presently Slovakia. Probably not existing anymore. I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell the name of the town that it was located in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hungarian name was Dunaszerdahely. D-U-N-A-S-Z-E-R-D-E-H-E-L-Y. I think that's close enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the first time when you knew what was going on, when trouble had started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I've got to think about it. Well, the trouble started because the Hungarians-- when the Germans invaded Sudetenland, the Hungarians invaded our area. And they immediately-- it was a question of Jewish rights. There were businesses taken away from us. Jews could not own their own business, and we had to sustain ourselves the best we could. But we managed somehow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you sustain yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, as a young boy, was able to get a head of a camera, and I was trying to take pictures for the peasants, for their weddings, and so forth and so on. They didn't have any bar mitzvahs. Otherwise I would have taken a picture of that also. But it was sustainable. We had-- had a little piece of land that we were able to grow our own stuff. And it was OK. It wasn't-- it wasn't very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- who was the main breadwinner of the family at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to live from the savings, really. There was nobody-- nobody that was such as a breadwinner. But when you had a general store, we were able to put away some stuff for ourselves, like soles for our shoes, that somebody needed to resole shoes, so they should have leather for shoes, to give it to the boot maker to be able to make shoes. I remember an incident where somebody turned us in, that we were black marketing shoe leather. And on the outside of our house, we had a cellar-like structure that was covered with a shed. So there was, between the roof of the shed and the cellar-- which was made out of dirt-- was room, and my grandmother was hiding some shoe leather over there. And one shabbos afternoon, the gendarme, the Hungarian police, came over. Three of them came over. And they were on a friendly basis, and they said that they found-- somebody reported us, that we were hiding some merchandise. And it's against the law to hold that, and so forth and so on, that we were black marketing it. And they were looking all over-- they couldn't find it. Finally, they went up to the shed, and they were looking all over. And I was standing on the outside, and my mother was standing next to me, looking up at the police looking for that stuff, for the contraband stuff. And he picked up a board in the shed, and he saw it-- he saw the leather there. He took a look at my mother, and my mother went like that. He covered it up and he walked out. He did not do nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever find out who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was one of the local police that we knew. And-- who turned us in? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who informed--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first feel the effects of the Nazis, the Germans themselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't feel the effects of the Germans, but there were-- there were Hungarian laws they were following pretty closely, that the Jews had to be identified with yellow stars, and we had to wear them at all times. And if you didn't wear the stars, you were punished. So these things were going on, and as the time was going on, it was getting worse and worse. But we got acclimated to the situation, and we had no real problems until the day came when they told us that they have to take us away from home. And if you are ready for that, I would like to tell you how it happened. The one-- I remember it was the last day of Pesach in 1944. We came home from shul, and we had dinner. After dinner, I walked over to my girlfriend's house, Etta, which was a couple doors away from us. And we were in the backyard talking about ourselves, probably-- having fun. And the police chief's wife-- the police chief lived directly across the street from my present wife's house. And the police chief's wife came over to me-- to us, to talk to us. And she says, I have some news that I would like you to relay to the-- to a fellow by the name of Juskovich. Now, Juskovich was very close to the authorities. He was a very wealthy man. He was doing a lot of good for the Jews and for non-Jews. And she says, the information that I would like to relay to you-- that an order came from Budapest that on Tuesday-- this was on a Saturday. Then on Tuesday, all the Jews have to assemble at the town square to be transported to a ghetto in Munkacs. I remember, I went over to Juskovich, and they were having dinner. Mrs. Juskovich was there, and their two sons. And I told them that I have a message from the police chief's wife. He says, Ari, don't say anything to anybody. Let me see what I can find out. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And I'll take it further from there. That was on a Saturday. And Tuesday, they had to take away-- the Jews were taken away already. It just so happened that I became 21 in 1944, in March of 1944, so I was eligible for military service. And an order came that I should report to the military service. At that time, the Jews did not serve in the military, but they were an arm of the military for labor. I left on Monday morning. They were supposed to take away everybody from the community on Tuesday. I remember a neighbor picked me up in front of the house with a wagon and horses to take me to the railroad station. My mother's face-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] The last time I saw her-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] --by the window, sadly waving to me and throwing me kisses. [CRYING] That was the last time I saw her. I know they took them away the next day. I don't know the details, but I know they took them to Munkacs. And there, they kept them for a few-- for several days, until they loaded them on the trains and they took them to Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [CRYING] In the military service, I went to Nagybanya.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- I had clothing. I had a wooden-- what is it called? Foot locker that was packed with clothes. Everything was in order. It was clothes, and change of underwear, and everything that-- and good boots and good shoes. And I went to Nagybanya-- if you want me to spell Nagybanya, that was the headquarters to that, spelled N-A-G-Y, capital B-A-N-Y-A. Basic training wasn't too bad. It was like military boot camp. We got up in the morning, we were marching, and disciplined, and marching, and singing, and marching, and marching. And then, during the day, when they had nothing to do with us, they made us carry stones up a hill. And then when the stones-- all the big stones were up the hill already, they made us carry it down and then carry it up again. So that was the form of exercise. But everybody was treated equally. I mean, it wasn't too bad. Then, from Nagybanya, we were there several weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a name-- a name used for the military?

speaker: interviewee
text: Munka Szalot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-U-N-K-A, capital S-Z-A-L-O-T. It's close enough. I think I spelled it properly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were distinguished between the military and ourselves. We did not have any uniforms. We had our own clothing. The only thing that we had to wear was the yellow armband on the left side. We were with Hungarian-- with the Hungarian Army-- under the Hungarian army. And we had the same officers as the regular army had. From after basic training, we went to Budakalasz, which is a suburb of Budapest. And Budakalasz is spelled B-U-D-A, capital K-O-L-A-S-Z. We were stationed in a-- I don't know whether it was a school. I think it was a big saloon, a big area that was requisitioned to house the group that we were there. We were involved in building antiaircraft installations. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were treated properly in the beginning. We worked. We came back. They fed us. We were exercising. I especially had a good-- a good job there, because I was working in the office, so I did not have to do any heavy labor. And I was helped by the office people that I was working with, which was civilians. And it wasn't bad until the end, when the Germans came over already. And the Nyilas party, which was the same as the SS Jugend, but the Hungarian-- Hungarian fascist party, and they tried to agitate and-- and tried to get a hold of us, and threatening us and so forth and so on. And towards the end already-- this was, I think, November or December 1944-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They herded us together in a schoolyard, because they were already working to take us out of Budapest, of Budakalasz, to take us into Germany, and to make our life threatened, or whatever. For whatever purposes, they herded us into a schoolyard, and they were torturing us there. We were sleeping on the outside. They had a couple of Jewish families that they did not touch from that area yet. They didn't take them to Auschwitz yet. And they had them in the schoolyard at that same time. And they had a lot of fun, these Nyilas, and the Germans had a lot of fun with us. They made us sweep the schoolyard with our hands. And anybody that left a piece of dirt on the ground had to lick it up with their tongue. And then one time, they brought in a-- a German truck came in and pulled into the school yard. And we were huddled in one area. They pulled up, and they dropped-- the back dropped from the truck, and they had a machine gun with three SS there. And they told us, now you guys have to realize this is the end. That wasn't the end, but it was the beginning of the end.
